title: Rationality: What It Is, Why It Seems Scarce, Why It Matters
author: Steven Pinker
excerpt: Steven Pinker makes the case that rationality is not a innate human birthright but a set of cognitive tools that must be learned—and that mastering them is essential not just for individual flourishing but for the survival of civilization itself.
keyIdeas: Rationality is a toolkit of reasoning strategies, not a single ability
Logic, probability, and critical thinking are learnable skills
People are not irrational—they are rational in different contexts
Rationality matters because irrationality has real-world consequences
keyTakeaways: Understand the tools of logic and avoid common fallacies
Seek out disconfirming evidence rather than confirmation
Recognize that rationality is a collective achievement, not an individual one
whoShouldRead: Anyone interested in improving their reasoning abilities
Students of psychology, philosophy, and cognitive science
Fans of Kahneman and Tetlock who want the philosophical foundations
People concerned about the state of public discourse and misinformation
whoShouldSkip: Readers looking for quick practical tips (this is more conceptual)
Those who find Pinker's style overly combative or dismissive
